bubbleSort.name: Bubble Sort
bubbleSort.description: Repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order, causing larger elements to 'bubble up' to the end.
bubbleSort.whenToUse: Primarily educational. Acceptable for very small arrays or nearly-sorted data where simplicity matters more than performance.

steps.sorted: Element {{v}} is in its final position

selectionSort.name: Selection Sort
selectionSort.description: Divides the array into sorted and unsorted regions. Repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted region and places it at the beginning.
selectionSort.whenToUse: Simple to implement. Use for small arrays where write operations are costly (minimizes swaps). Not suitable for large datasets.

steps.findMin: Finding minimum in unsorted region starting at index {{i}}

insertionSort.name: Insertion Sort
insertionSort.description: Builds the sorted array one element at a time by inserting each new element into its correct position within the already-sorted portion.
insertionSort.whenToUse: Excellent for small arrays or nearly-sorted data. Used as a subroutine in more complex algorithms like Timsort.

mergeSort.name: Merge Sort
mergeSort.description: A divide-and-conquer algorithm that recursively splits the array in half, sorts each half, then merges them back together in sorted order.
mergeSort.whenToUse: Reliable O(n log n) for any input. Preferred for linked lists, stable sorting, and external sorting of large datasets.

quickSort.name: Quick Sort
quickSort.description: Selects a pivot element, partitions the array into elements less than and greater than the pivot, then recursively sorts each partition.
quickSort.whenToUse: Average O(n log n) with small constant factor. Typically fastest in practice for in-memory sorting. Widely used in system libraries.

heapSort.name: Heap Sort
heapSort.description: Builds a max-heap from the array, then repeatedly extracts the maximum element and places it at the end, rebuilding the heap each time.
heapSort.whenToUse: Guaranteed O(n log n) worst case with O(1) space. Use when worst-case performance is critical and space is constrained.

countingSort.name: Counting Sort
countingSort.description: Counts the occurrences of each value, then reconstructs the sorted array by iterating over the count array.
countingSort.whenToUse: O(n+k) time for non-negative integers with small range k. Ideal for sorting integers, characters, or categorical data.

radixSort.name: Radix Sort
radixSort.description: Sorts numbers digit by digit from least significant to most significant, using a stable sort (like counting sort) for each digit pass.
radixSort.whenToUse: O(d·n) where d is number of digits. Efficient for integers and strings with bounded length. Used in suffix array construction.

shellSort.name: Shell Sort
shellSort.description: A generalisation of insertion sort that sorts elements far apart first using a shrinking gap sequence, then finishes with a standard insertion sort pass.
shellSort.whenToUse: Good practical performance for medium-sized arrays. Faster than insertion sort in most cases. Used in embedded systems and as a subroutine in more complex algorithms.

steps.gap: New gap h = {{h}} — sorting sub-sequences with stride {{h}}

linearSearch.name: Linear Search
linearSearch.description: Sequentially checks each element until the target is found or the array ends. Works on both sorted and unsorted arrays.
linearSearch.whenToUse: Simple O(n) search for small or unsorted arrays. Best when you only search occasionally and the list is short.

binarySearch.name: Binary Search
binarySearch.description: On a sorted array, repeatedly halves the search space by comparing the target with the middle element.
binarySearch.whenToUse: O(log n) search for sorted arrays. Use when the data is sorted and searches are frequent (libraries, databases, competitive programming).

bfs.name: Breadth-First Search
bfs.description: Explores a graph level by level, visiting all neighbors before moving deeper. Uses a queue to track the next node to visit.
bfs.whenToUse: Finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs. Level-order traversal. Useful in social networks, GPS navigation, web crawlers.

dfs.name: Depth-First Search
dfs.description: Explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. Uses a stack (or recursion) to track nodes to visit.
dfs.whenToUse: Cycle detection, topological sort, path finding, maze solving. Memory-efficient for deep graphs compared to BFS.

arrayOps.name: Array Operations
arrayOps.description: Fundamental operations on arrays: access, insertion, deletion, and search. The foundation of most data structure implementations.
arrayOps.whenToUse: Arrays provide O(1) random access and are cache-friendly. Use when index-based access is frequent and size is known.

doublyLinkedList.name: Doubly Linked List
doublyLinkedList.description: A sequence of nodes where each node holds a value and pointers to both the next and previous nodes, enabling efficient traversal in both directions.
doublyLinkedList.whenToUse: When bidirectional traversal or O(1) removal at both ends is needed. Used in browser history, LRU caches, and undo/redo stacks.

linkedList.name: Singly Linked List
linkedList.description: A sequence of nodes where each node stores a value and a pointer to the next node. Allows efficient insertion and deletion at any point.
linkedList.whenToUse: When frequent insertions/deletions are needed and random access is not required. Foundation for stacks, queues, and hash table chaining.

stack.name: Stack
stack.description: A Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure. Elements are added and removed from the same end (top).
stack.whenToUse: Function call management, undo operations, expression evaluation, backtracking algorithms (DFS), and browser history.

queue.name: Queue
queue.description: A First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure. Elements are added at the rear and removed from the front.
queue.whenToUse: Task scheduling, BFS, print spoolers, messaging systems, and any scenario requiring ordered processing.

binaryTree.name: Binary Tree Traversals
binaryTree.description: Three ways to visit all nodes: in-order (left-root-right), pre-order (root-left-right), and post-order (left-right-root).
binaryTree.whenToUse: In-order gives sorted output for BST. Pre-order for copying/serializing trees. Post-order for deletion and evaluation.

bst.name: Binary Search Tree
bst.description: A binary tree where the left subtree contains only nodes with values less than the root, and the right subtree only nodes greater.
bst.whenToUse: Efficient O(log n) search, insert, delete when balanced. Foundation for sets, maps, and ordered data structures.

hashTable.name: Hash Table
hashTable.description: Maps keys to values using a hash function. Provides average O(1) insert, delete, and lookup with chaining for collision handling.
hashTable.whenToUse: When you need fast key-value lookups. Used in caches, databases, compilers, and most modern language runtimes.

minHeap.name: Min-Heap
minHeap.description: A complete binary tree where every parent node is smaller than its children. The root is always the minimum element.
minHeap.whenToUse: Priority queues, Dijkstra's algorithm, heap sort, scheduling systems. O(log n) insert and O(1) min access.

fibonacci.name: Fibonacci
fibonacci.description: Computes Fibonacci numbers using memoization (top-down) or tabulation (bottom-up), showing how DP eliminates redundant computation.
fibonacci.whenToUse: Demonstrates the core DP concept. Memoization vs tabulation trade-offs apply to many optimization problems.

knapsack.name: 0/1 Knapsack
knapsack.description: Finds the maximum value subset of items that fits in a weight-capacity knapsack, where each item can be taken or left.
knapsack.whenToUse: Resource allocation, budget optimization, cargo loading. Classic DP problem with many real-world variants.

lcs.name: Longest Common Subsequence
lcs.description: Finds the longest subsequence present in both strings in the same relative order (not necessarily contiguous).
lcs.whenToUse: Diff tools (git), spell checkers, bioinformatics (DNA comparison), version control systems.

lis.name: Longest Increasing Subsequence
lis.description: Finds the longest subsequence of a sequence in which the elements are in strictly increasing order.
lis.whenToUse: Patience sorting, stock trading problems, chain problems, patience game analysis.

coinChange.name: Coin Change
coinChange.description: Finds the minimum number of coins needed to make a given amount, given an array of coin denominations.
coinChange.whenToUse: Currency systems, vending machines, any problem requiring minimum resources to reach a target.
